So let's go through that now, the main body. And it starts with number one, and I mentioned this to you before, and it's absolutely crucial. And that's that you do not, under any circumstances, front load. Okay? You do not want to front load. You don't want to be listening to benefit after benefit after benefit, because it gives you nowhere to go after they say, let me think about it. And again, we've been through this a couple of times now, so I'll just move on. Now number two, and this is almost a byproduct of number one, is that your script should be very short. The body should be no more than three quarters of a typewritten page at most. And when you're done with your script and you read it back to yourself, you should have given no more than two or three very powerful distinctions about your product. You don't want to have 10, because that means you're rambled on, essentially you front loaded. And remember this, we don't sell features. Now I know this is basic influence, one on one, we sell benefits, not features, right? But I see people all the time that aren't training the straight line, they think they're selling benefits, but they still get caught up in the features. Okay? Now remember this, as a salesperson, when you present to someone, if you list feature after feature after feature, what ends up happening is the client simply tunes out. People don't buy things because they have wonderful features. They buy because they imagine the features benefiting them somehow in their own life. You say to someone, the computer does ABCD, well, all they want to know is kind of make them feel good when they get it. Well, they'll be able to watch the TV shows they want. Well, they'll be able to surf the internet easily and quickly without getting caught up with pop-ups and whatnot, right? So don't get caught up in selling feature after feature after feature. Focus on your benefits. And you don't want to have more than three at the most in the main body of your presentation. If you have more than that, you've front-loaded. You've given yourself a huge problem down the straight line when they still wanted to think about it and you have nothing intelligent left to say. Now number three, the flow of the presentation must be seamless. In other words, it has to flow in a way where there are certain chunks that sound really great and powerful. And there are other stopping off points where the client has a chance to catch his breath or interact and process the information that you've just given him. For instance, you make a powerful statement and you make another powerful statement. And then you get another powerful statement. Eventually those three powerful statements start to blend together and they lose their power. So what you want to do is after you make a powerful statement, you want to have that point home by saying to your client, you follow me so far? Does that make sense to you? You're with me so far? Do you get it? Essentially, you want to give the person a chance to say yes, to agree with you, and also to process the information and to catch their breath a bit. Now that's important on two levels. First, so the person doesn't simply just tune out because you've been preaching at them like a talking head. Again, you want this exchange to be more of a dialogue than a monologue. And secondly, because you want to get the prospect actually into the habit of saying yes. In other words, and this is very subtle, when you were in a sale situation, the prospect keeps saying no, no, no, no, no. It's a heck of a lot harder to get them to say yes when the time actually comes to close the sale. And this goes back to something you've probably learned in one of the earlier modules by now. And it's called consistency, meaning that people want to remain consistent with things that they've said out loud and actions they've taken in public. So if you get someone into the habit of saying yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, the chances are better than not that when you actually ask them for the order, they're predisposed to saying yes. Now I want you to understand that the straight line system is far more advanced than simply thinking that if we can get people into the habit of saying yes, they'll be able to easily close them at the end. It's not like that. What I'm saying though is that what you don't want to do is to get your client into the habit of saying no. Now let me give you an example of this. When you're in the middle of a script and you say to someone, does the idea make sense to you? So do you like the idea? And he says, yeah, it does. Then you're in rapport and you're actually moving them down the straight line. But if you say to someone, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And they say, no, it doesn't. Then you try to go forward down the straight line. Then you're out of rapport. See what the client feels or he thinks and say, hey, this guy doesn't care about what I say. All he wants to do is ram this product down my throat. You get it? So it's very important that when you ask someone, do you like the idea? Does it make sense to you? Do you follow me so far? You get a positive yes statement from them. And if you don't have one, you have to backtrack, essentially look backwards and resell that point and get them to agree with you. If you don't, there is no way you can move forward and close the sale. Now number four, this has to do with the use of what I call colloquialisms. And what this means is that whenever possible, you want to be speaking in layman's terms as opposed to using overly official industry buzzwords to the point where you sound like you're using a canned script. You want to sound natural as if you're speaking to a friend that you want to connect with, not just logically, but emotionally as well. Now remember, you got to make sure that you balance that out by sounding like an expert. So there is a balance there. It's not like you're saying, hey, buddy, how's it going? Yeah, this is a really great product. I'm not talking about that at all. I want you to sound like an educated expert that speaks to you. Just touch over your client's knowledge base, but you're still in that sort of friendly rapport where you're an educated expert slash friend speaking to someone that trusts you and trying to get into that level of certainty to that level where they feel good about it both logically and emotionally, and they feel empowered to buy as opposed to feeling a product being rammed down their throat and being sold to. Remember that. People do not like being sold to, but they love to buy. And that's that sort of balance you have to strike when you're that friendly expert that's leading them down the straight line speaking in casual but business-like terms at a level just slightly above their knowledge base. And when you're there in that kill zone, you'll see people will just simply will say, wow, yeah, Jordan, it makes sense. It sounds great because they just know you sound good and intellectually and emotionally, they just want to agree because they feel like they're with an expert so it can help them resolve their pain, get them what they want in life. And that's truly the key to the mix of tonality and words that allows you to take someone down the straight line quickly and elegantly from the open to the close and ultimately close everyone who's close. Now number five, this has to do with mastering the script through something called practice essentially by reading it out loud. And the way I like to do this is I work with another salesman and we practice back and forth with each other role playing. And we see which words are giving us trouble, which words are tongue twisters, any words that don't sound exactly right, that don't flow together, right? And we work back and forth until it flows absolutely seamlessly. So it's so easy to say that virtually anyone can say it. Essentially you want to get the script to the point where any salesperson, not just the top salesperson, but any salesperson, even the worst one can go through the script and still sound great, still sound like an expert. And just so you know, there's a little bit of artistry involved in this and it has to do with balance. In other words, the script has to be bound to the right number of syllables in each sentence, the right number of beats in each sentence. It's like a rhythm. And if something just rambles on and on, the humane is very attuned to this, it will simply just tune out. It's like when you're in the car, you're driving and you hear a radio station and the music is just off kilter in some way. You don't like it, what do you do? You change the station or you turn it off. Well, the same exact thing happens in selling situations. If you're selling it for some reason, your words just don't sound right, the beat is not pleasing to the ear. Your client will simply tune out and ultimately change your station, it means kick you out of the sale situation, you get it? Okay? So it's crucial to sound good and to make sure that your words are pleasing to the ear and the way you achieve that is through practice. And by working on your script and editing it and editing it again, going back and forth with another person until every word flows like butter. Number six, and this is about remembering that you're always closing. Not necessarily closing the sale itself, but you're closing the person that you're talking to and wanting to listen to your next paragraph or even your next word that you're saying. It's about keeping the prospect in the game, meaning open to being influenced. You're always trying to do that, to close them on keeping them in the game so they can be influenced. And the way you do that is by sounding great the entire time. And I want you to remember this from your tonality module, which I know you've all been through by now, that this starts, this process of capturing someone's attention and sounding good. It starts from those first few words you say. It's like, you know, hey John, how you doing today? It's boom, you're out there with power, sharpest attack, enthusiastic as hell and an expert in your field to establish right away in those first few words. So you wouldn't say, hi, is John there? You're like, hey, is John there? That's the powerful way to capture someone's attention and sound good. And lastly, number seven, and this is one that's really super critical to me personally, is that you need to make sure that your scripts are honest and ethical. You don't want to have any lies or ridiculous exaggerations in them. And really the question you need to ask yourself with every sentence that you create is, am I coming from a place of integrity? Am I coming from a place of ethics? Or am I starting to exaggerate a bit here and sort of misleading my client? If the answer is the latter, then you know it's time to pull back and rewrite that sentence. And by the way, if you're an owner or sales manager of a company and you put a script out into your sales force that's riddled with lies and exaggerations, they know it. And when they're out there hammering away on the phone or out into the field telling these lies and exaggerating, it starts to seep into the entire corporate culture and it corrupts it. Next thing you know, people are trying to rob and steal not only from their clients, but they're also robbing and stealing from you. It starts from the top down that corporate culture. So if you're putting unethical scripts out there, even with the slightest exaggerations, eventually, yeah, you might make a couple of extra bucks in the beginning, but eventually it will reverse itself very, very badly and end up eating your company up alive. So please, and I beg this of you, make sure that, yeah, your scripts need to be sexy, powerful, they got to deliver that necessary punch that creates those false lies. There's five core elements of the three tens and allows you to close everyone who's closeable, but they must be accurate and they must be ethical and they have to come from a place of absolute integrity. If you do that, you'll build a powerful sales force that will last forever.
